ACG Clinical Guideline: Hepatic Encephalopathy
  • Source: American College of Gastroenterology (ACG), Published March 3, 2026
  • Topic: Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Hepatic Encephalopathy in Cirrhosis
  • Authors: Jasmohan S. Bajaj et al.
Topics
  • Section 1: Introduction, Background, Burden of HE, and CHE/MHE (Covert/Minimal Hepatic Encephalopathy) - covering definitions, epidemiology, diagnostic testing strategies, and clinical relevance
  • Section 2: Overt HE (OHE) Management - inpatient treatment, prevention of recurrence, medication strategies (lactulose, rifaximin), and health information technology interventions
  • Section 3: Special Considerations - frailty/sarcopenia/nutrition, TIPS-related HE, liver transplant implications, and emerging therapies

The guideline uses GRADE methodology with strong and conditional recommendations, addressing 24 recommendations across the spectrum of HE care from diagnosis through transplant considerations.

Section 1: Introduction, Background, and CHE/MHE Diagnosis
1. Introduction and Methodology
  • ACG Practice Parameters Committee convened expert writing group to develop HE guidelines
  • PICO questions guided literature search in PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library
  • GRADE process assessed quality of evidence (high, moderate, low, very low)
  • Strength of recommendation: strong (desirable effects clearly outweigh undesirable) or conditional (tradeoffs less certain)
  • Key concepts included where GRADE not applied (expert opinion, definitions)
2. Background: Definition and Conceptual Framework
  • HE definition: "brain dysfunction resulting from liver impairment or portosystemic shunting, presenting as spectrum of neurological and psychiatric manifestations"
  • Three villages framework: (1) multiple pathogenetic factors within patient, (2) negative impact on patients/families/healthcare systems, (3) multiple specialties/practices needed for management
  • Five axes for HE assessment: type, grade/severity, time course, precipitating factors, social infrastructure (newly added)
  • Social infrastructure critical for preventing readmissions: social/family support, financial support for medication adherence, institutional support for follow-up
3. Burden of HE: Incidence, Prevalence, and Cost
Incidence
  • HE now first decompensating event among cirrhosis patients
  • 1-year incidence: 10% for Child A, 25% for Child B patients with cirrhosis and portal hypertension
  • 5-year incidence: 3.8% to 43.7% (varies by population and coding reliability)
  • Veterans Affairs cohort with cirrhosis + portal hypertension/APRI >2.0: higher incidence
Prevalence
  • Estimated 118,000 individuals with HE in US (based on 260 million adults × 40% of decompensated cirrhosis developing HE)
  • Prevalence in CHE/MHE: 20%–80% of patients with cirrhosis and no prior OHE, depending on hepatic impairment (highest in Child-Pugh C)
Cost
  • Inpatient HE hospitalization costs: rose from $38,897 (2007) to $49,391 (2017)
  • HE most common cause of readmission among cirrhosis patients: 21%–53% 90-day readmission rate
  • Healthcare cost after initial HE hospitalization: $18,457–$20,030 per person per month (5.7–6.9 months follow-up)
Caregiver Impact
  • Significant caregiver burden reported regarding schedule, personal health, sense of entrapment
  • Caregivers report debt, food insecurity, difficulty paying bills, evictions
  • Health-related quality of life measures impaired in caregivers
4. CHE and MHE: Definitions and Clinical Relevance
Definitions
  • MHE (Minimal Hepatic Encephalopathy): subclinical form identified only through specialized neuropsychological or neurophysiological testing
  • CHE (Covert Hepatic Encephalopathy): combines MHE + grade 1 HE by West Haven criteria
  • OHE (Overt Hepatic Encephalopathy): grades 2–4, clinically obvious
Clinical Manifestations
  • Attention deficit affecting attention span and shift → working memory deficit, impaired learning
  • Reduced motor coordination and psychomotor speed → prone to falls and self-injury
  • Deficits in visuospatial orientation, response inhibition, speed of information processing → navigation issues, unfit to drive
  • Higher risk for incidental OHE and hospitalization
  • Associated with accelerated cirrhosis progression and death
  • Decreased quality of life
Motor Abnormalities in CHE
  • Bradykinesia, rigidity, parkinsonian-like extrapyramidal signs may be observed
  • Asterixis (flapping tremor) can occur but isolated asterixis without behavioral changes ≠ OHE
  • Recent regulatory updates: asterixis without behavioral abnormalities should not be considered or treated as OHE
5. Testing Strategies for CHE/MHE
When to Test (Prioritization Criteria)

Patients with cirrhosis and 1 or more of:

  • Hypoalbuminemia
  • Decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, variceal bleeding, jaundice)
  • Clinically significant portal hypertension or portosystemic shunts (particularly if large, ≥8 mm)
  • Nonspecific cognitive complaints: concentration difficulties, impaired QoL, navigation issues/traffic violations, falls
  • Difficulty operating heavy machinery
  • Commercial drivers/pilots
Testing Options
Test Time Tested Domains CHE/MHE Prevalence
Stroop EncephalApp (Full) 5–15 min Psychomotor speed, cognitive flexibility 45%–55%
QuickStroop 1–2 min Psychomotor speed 45%–55%
Animal Naming Test 1 min Memory, verbal retrieval 30%–40%
Critical Flicker Frequency — Integrity of visual tract, reaction time 40%–55%
Single-Test vs. Two-Test Strategy
  • Recommendation: Single-test strategy over 2-test combination (conditional recommendation, very low certainty)
  • Rationale: When tested in real-world, 2-test strategy showed no improved accuracy for incidental OHE prediction; lower sensitivity makes it less practical for screening
Serum Ammonia
  • Recommendation: Against using serum ammonia levels alone to diagnose CHE/MHE (conditional recommendation, very low certainty)
  • No laboratory test sufficient to diagnose or rule out CHE
Alternative Diagnoses
  • Large proportion of patients with cirrhosis and cognitive complaints have conditions OTHER than MHE
  • Should assess for: sleep apnea, alcohol-related conditions, mild cognitive impairment, dementia, mood disorders, PTSD
  • Patients with isolated delayed memory issues or isolated sleep issues often have overlapping explanations
6. Treatment for CHE/MHE
First-Line Therapy: Lactulose
  • Improvement in neuropsychological tests reported in multiple RCTs
  • Higher MHE reversal rate vs. control/placebo
  • Quality of life improves after treatment
  • Decreased rate of incidental OHE (though evidence on mortality lacking)
  • Theragnostic approach: Short trial of lactulose (4–8 weeks) with symptom-based response can provide diagnostic and therapeutic justification
Rifaximin
  • Similar results to lactulose in comparisons vs. placebo
  • Noninferiority margin maintained in RCT against lactulose
  • Helps with driving simulation performance
  • Cost and third-payer approval may limit use
Second-Line Agents (Considerations)
  • BCAA/LOLA: if sarcopenia/frailty
  • Zinc: if low serum levels
  • L-carnitine: if concomitant skeletal muscle cramps
  • No firm evidence that second-line agents decrease incidental OHE rate
Key Concept: Treatment Decision
  • Main beneficial outcome: improvement in daily function and quality of life
  • Decision to treat and choice of medication should involve shared decision-making with patient/caregiver
7. OHE Management: Decision for Outpatient vs Inpatient
Guiding Principles

1. Severity of mental status changes dictates need for emergency room evaluation and hospitalization; CHE and selected patients with grade 2 HE could be managed as outpatient

2. First episode of mental status changes: evaluation in ED may be necessary to exclude other etiology; typical presentation consistent with prior HE episodes could be managed as outpatient if HE grade ≤2

3. Suspicion of precipitants: infection, bleeding, dehydration, or other precipitant requiring evaluation/treatment → need ED evaluation and possible hospitalization

4. Reliable support/supervision: if patient is agitated or unlikely to comply, outpatient management not an option

Factors Supporting Outpatient Management
  • CHE or selected grade 2 HE without precipitating factors requiring acute intervention
  • Ability of family/patient to manage in outpatient setting
  • No suspicion of infection, bleeding, dehydration
  • Close outpatient follow-up available
Factors Requiring Hospitalization
  • Grade 3–4 HE (significant impairment of consciousness)
  • New-onset mental status changes requiring exclusion of other etiology
  • Presence of precipitating factors (infection, GI bleeding, electrolyte disorders)
  • No reliable family/caregiver support
  • Risk of aspiration
8. Inpatient Management of OHE
Recommendations (5–9)

Recommendation 5: In patients with HE, suggest against routine testing of serum ammonia to guide HE treatment decisions (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 6: In patients with cirrhosis and confusion without new-onset focal neurologic deficits, suggest against routine brain imaging (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 7: In patients with OHE, recommend treatment with lactulose to improve patient outcomes and prevent recurrence of OHE episodes (strong recommendation, moderate certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 8: In patients with OHE, suggest treatment with high-volume (e.g., 4 L) polyethylene glycol preparations as an alternative option to lactulose therapy (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 9: In patients with acute OHE, suggest adding rifaximin to lactulose therapy vs. lactulose therapy alone (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Key Concepts (9–14)

Key Concept 9: Clinical scores (WH criteria, GCS) identify patients with high-grade HE requiring appropriate triage/transfer to SDU/MICU to prevent complications such as aspiration pneumonia

Key Concept 10: Thorough evaluation for bleeding, infection, sedating medications, and toxic/metabolic abnormalities necessary to identify/treat precipitating factors

Key Concept 11: Normal blood ammonia level in patients with altered mental status should prompt consideration of other potential diagnoses

Key Concept 12: If patient does not recover mental status after 48–72 hours of adequate HE therapy and precipitating factor reversal, evaluation for alternative causes, inadequately addressed precipitants, or shunts should be considered

Key Concept 13: Multidisciplinary medication reconciliation at hospital discharge necessary to ensure appropriate prophylactic therapies and minimize harmful medications

Clinical Assessment and Grading
West Haven Criteria
  • Grade 0: No detectable changes in personality, behavior, or intellect
  • Grade 1: Mild confusion, decreased consciousness, insomnia, mild personality change, shortened attention span
  • Grade 2: Lethargy, disorientation to time, obvious personality change, drowsiness
  • Grade 3: Somnolence to semi-stupor, disoriented to person, marked confusion, inappropriate behavior
  • Grade 4: Coma, no response to verbal stimuli
Glasgow Coma Scale
  • Used for patients with significant impairment of level of consciousness
  • Assesses: eye opening, verbal response, motor response
  • Scores range from 3 (deep coma) to 15 (fully alert)
Hepatic Encephalopathy Scoring Algorithm (HESA)
  • Validated across all grades of HE
  • Uses clinical and paper-pencil test incorporating attention, memory, psychomotor function
  • Point-based system for grading; ~20 minutes to administer
  • Used in research setting rather than clinical practice
Initial Evaluation Approach

1. Grade severity of mental status changes to triage level of care (outpatient vs. regular floor vs. SDU/MICU)

2. Determine if HE is cause of mental status changes (with or without other conditions causing cognitive changes)

3. Identify precipitating/contributing factors requiring treatment: constipation, GI bleeding, infections, hyponatremia, dehydration/diuretic overdose

Laboratory Evaluation
  • Blood cultures, ascites culture, urine culture; stool/sputum if clinical suspicion
  • Venous ammonia NOT routinely measured: not diagnostic for HE; can be used to rule out HE if patient stuporous/comatose and ammonia normal
  • Therapy should NOT be adjusted based on ammonia level, but rather clinical improvement
  • Sample handling and processing affect blood ammonia values; unreliable for diagnosis/treatment decisions
  • Ammonia levels do NOT correlate with HE severity or prognosis
Brain Imaging
  • Routine CT/MRI does not diagnose HE
  • Limited value in patients without focal neurological deficits, seizures, or multiple prior episodes
  • Useful to investigate: acute/chronic subdural hematoma, subarachnoid bleeding, stroke, brain lesion
  • Separate from research-related multimodal imaging (role in determining pathophysiology)
Precipitating Factors to Evaluate
  • Infection: most common cause of hospitalization in cirrhosis and common HE precipitant; cultures of blood, ascites, urine; empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics if symptoms/lab findings suggest infection
  • GI bleeding: requires evaluation and treatment
  • Electrolyte disorders: hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypercalcemia
  • Acute kidney injury: worsens HE
  • Constipation: increases ammonia absorption
  • Dehydration/diuretic overdose: volume depletion
  • Sedating medications: opiates, benzodiazepines, gabapentin, pregabalin, sleep medications
9. Medications for Inpatient HE Treatment
First-Line: Lactulose
Mechanism of Action
  • Acidifies gut lumen → traps ammonia as ammonium ions → prevents absorption, increases elimination via stool
  • Presumed prebiotic effect: inhibits ammoniagenic bacteria, promotes acidophilic bacteria (lactobacilli) lacking urease
  • Inhibits intestinal glutamine uptake → decreased ammoniagenesis
Dosing and Administration
  • Grade 2 HE: oral lactulose 10–20 g (15–30 mL) every 2 hours until 2 soft bowel movements, then reduce to 2–4 times daily to maintain 2–3 BMs daily
  • Grade 3–4 HE (unable to swallow or aspiration risk): nasogastric tube administration OR lactulose enema (200 g lactulose or 300 mL solution + 700 mL water/normal saline, retain 30–60 minutes, repeat every 4–6 hours until improvement)
Complications of Overuse
  • Perianal skin irritation/infection
  • Ileus
  • Aspiration
  • Dehydration
  • Hypernatremia
  • Hypokalemia (can become precipitant for HE recurrence)
Alternative: Polyethylene Glycol (PEG)
  • PEG 3350-electrolyte solution (e.g., 4 L) led to more rapid HE resolution than standard lactulose in RCT
  • Consider in patients with abdominal discomfort, gas distension, or ileus from lactulose
Enemas
  • Tap water enema: rapid access and wide availability
  • Acidifying agents (lactose, lactitol) superior to tap water enemas for acute HE management
Rifaximin
  • Nonabsorbable antibiotic affecting ammonia production by modulating intestinal bacterial composition
  • Established for HE recurrence prevention; impact on acute treatment less defined
  • Data suggest rifaximin + lactulose more effective than lactulose alone for inpatient treatment
  • RCT showed combination resulted in higher rate of complete HE reversal, reduction in hospital stay, and mortality
Albumin
  • Investigated as adjunct to lactulose
  • Limited data showing increased rate of complete HE reversal and reduction in mortality
  • Role remains unclear
Treatment of Precipitating Factors
  • Critical because addresses underlying cause and may reverse HE
  • Concomitant disorders affecting mental status: hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, thiamine deficiency
  • Hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, sepsis can independently cause metabolic encephalopathy or precipitate HE
  • Thorough investigation for infection: diagnostic paracentesis essential
  • Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics only if symptoms/lab findings suggest active infection; tailor or discontinue based on culture results
10. HE Management in ICU Setting
Special Considerations
  • For patients with ACLF (acute on chronic liver failure): management addressed in 2022 ACG guidelines
  • For patients requiring ICU without ACLF: apply general principles with additional considerations
Lactulose Use in Intubated/Sedated Patients
  • Dosing based on bowel movements (mental status assessment not possible)
  • Fecal management system: hold lactulose if >300 mL stool per day
  • Monitor for hypernatremia suggesting dehydration
  • Prevent aspiration
Rifaximin Use in ICU
  • Limited value, especially if patient already on broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • Double-blind trial showed reversal of OHE similar in antibiotics alone vs. antibiotics + rifaximin groups
Nontraditional Therapies
  • Ammonia dialyzable: early RRT for hyperammonemia (>100 μmol/L) may be beneficial in acute liver failure or urea cycle disorders; insufficient data for cirrhosis
  • High-volume plasma exchange: improves HE and survival benefit in acute liver failure; not demonstrated in cirrhosis
  • Albumin dialysis (MARS): reduces HE grade in cirrhosis, potentially bridges to transplant; further studies needed
11. Nonresponse to HE Therapy
Definition
  • No improvement in mental status with first-line treatment within 24–48 hours
  • Persistence of delirium after 48 hours despite optimal bowel movements and treatment of precipitating factors
Evaluation Approach (Figure 9)
Step 1: Explore Alternate Diagnosis
  • Ammonia levels checked haphazardly; if normal, alternative causes of altered mentation should be investigated
  • Low/normal ammonia has negative predictive value for HE as cause
  • Consider: head trauma, falls, seizures, surgeries
Step 2: Determine Undiagnosed Precipitants
  • Monitor blood work for electrolyte disorders, renal failure
  • Thorough history regarding central-acting medications or recreational drugs (verify with urine drug screen, phosphatidylethanolamine testing)
  • Clinically aware of overt GI bleeding
  • Search for infectious etiology if no response within 48 hours of lactulose ± rifaximin: pan culture, paracentesis, chest X-ray
  • Cross-sectional imaging can help identify prior TIPS placement vs. spontaneous portosystemic shunts
Step 3: Identify Impact of Portosystemic Shunts
  • Persistent encephalopathy despite appropriate medical treatment → evaluate with cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI)
  • Incidence of SPSSs in patients with HE refractory to medical therapy: 46%–71% vs. 14% without SPSS
  • May require closure (see Prevention section)
12. Prevention of HE Recurrence
Recommendations (10–16)

Recommendation 10: After initial episode of OHE, recommend lactulose titrated to 2–3 soft bowel movements daily as outpatient first-line therapy for prevention of HE recurrence (strong recommendation, high certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 11: In patients treated with lactulose for HE, suggest using Bristol Stool Scale with bowel movement frequency for outpatient titration to reduce readmissions (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 12: In patients with cirrhosis and OHE, suggest rifaximin therapy in outpatient setting to prevent HE recurrence (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 13: In patients with OHE on lactulose maintenance therapy who experience recurrent episodes of HE, recommend addition of rifaximin treatment (strong recommendation, high certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 14: In patients with OHE and persistent symptoms despite lactulose and rifaximin therapy, suggest addition of zinc supplementation in those with low blood zinc levels (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 15: Suggest implementing health information technology interventions (Patient Buddy App, electronic clinical reminders) when feasible to optimize HE management (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 16: Suggest shunt embolization in patients with refractory HE on optimized medical therapy who have adequate hepatic function and no contraindications (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Key Concepts for Prevention

Key Concept 14: Serum ammonia levels should NOT be used to guide initiation or titration of HE therapies or evaluation of HE recurrence in outpatient setting

Key Concept 15: Thorough review of medication lists to identify/minimize therapies affecting mental status or encephalopathy risk: opiates, benzodiazepines, gabapentin/pregabalin, sleep medications (zolpidem, zaleplon, eszopiclone), proton pump inhibitors

Key Concept 16: HE therapy may be deescalated and potentially discontinued in stepwise fashion in patients who clinically recompensate

Key Concept 17: Oral and written advice to avoid driving should be given to patients/caregivers based on expert consensus in patients with recent (<3 months) episodes of OHE

Key Concept 18: All patients with HE and caregivers should receive HE-based educational materials (e.g., www.cirrhosiscare.ca)

Key Concept 19: Caregivers should be screened for caregiver burnout and provided necessary support

Risk of Recurrence
  • After index OHE episode: 40%–60% experience second episode within 1 year
  • Secondary prophylaxis indicated in all patients with cirrhosis who develop OHE
Medications for Prevention
Lactulose
  • Significantly decreases risk of HE recurrence vs. placebo
  • Titrated to 2–3 soft bowel movements daily for efficacy while avoiding diarrhea-associated hypovolemia
  • Adverse events generally mild: nausea, bloating, flatulence, diarrhea
  • Breakthrough episodes commonly attributed to nonadherence or dehydration
Bristol Stool Scale (BSS) Integration
  • Incorporation removes focus from 2–3 daily BM frequency to characteristics of BMs
  • Use of BSS shown to complement goal daily BMs in stabilizing lactulose regimens, improving patient adherence
  • Apps available for monitoring daily BM counts
  • AI-enabled apps using standardized BSS assessment provide just-in-time feedback on lactulose dosing sufficiency
  • BSS score ≥5 requires cutting down lactulose or adding rifaximin if already on lactulose
  • If BSS ≥5 before lactulose initiation, consider alternative nonlaxative therapies de novo
Rifaximin
  • Nonsystemic antibiotic with broad activity against aerobic/anaerobic bacteria, modulates intestinal microbiota
  • FDA-approved for reduction in risk of OHE recurrence in adults: 550 mg twice daily
  • RCT (299 patients, ≥2 OHE episodes in preceding 6 months, 91% on concomitant lactulose): 58% risk reduction in breakthrough OHE (NNT=4), 50% risk reduction in HE-related hospitalization (NNT=9) at 6 months
  • Systematic review and meta-analysis (17 RCTs): rifaximin beneficial for secondary prevention of HE
  • Post-hoc subgroup analysis: rifaximin + lactulose more effective than lactulose alone for preventing recurrence and HE-related hospitalization
  • Rifaximin monotherapy can maintain remission, lower hospitalization risk in small/retrospective studies; preponderance of evidence supports combination with lactulose
  • Generally safe and well-tolerated
Novel Rifaximin Preparation
  • Rifamycin SV MMX (solid soluble dispersion tablets) being investigated with higher water solubility, less reliance on bile acids for action
  • Phase 3 trial results pending
Zinc Supplementation
  • Role as cofactor in nitrogen metabolism; deficiency decreases urea cycle efficiency, implicated in HE pathogenesis
  • Overall efficacy results conflicting
  • Safe adjunctive therapy that may improve cognitive function when added to lactulose in HE
Health Information Technology Interventions
  • Electronic order sets for appropriate lactulose titration and rifaximin use: 40% reduction in 30-day readmissions
  • Active interruptive best practice alert-based strategy for rifaximin use: increased prescriptions from 52.6% to 71.1% on non-GI services; reduced readmission risk (sHR 0.77, 95% CI 0.67–0.95)
  • Patient Buddy App: increases adherence, reduces HE-related readmissions through communication between team and patients/families
Fecal Microbiota Transplant (FMT)
  • Enema-based FMT after antibiotics vs. standard of care: safety, reduction in HE recurrence at 6 and 12 months (phase 1 trial)
  • Capsule-based FMT without pre-FMT antibiotics: safety confirmed, paved way for larger trials
  • THEMATIC trial (phase 2a dose-ranging double-blind): FMT safe, associated with reduced HE recurrence; specific route/dose not contributory
  • All FMT for HE should be confined to clinical trials at this time
Medication Review and Deprescribing
  • CNS depressant medications exacerbate neurotoxicity of ammonia
  • Opioids: impair intestinal motility → more ammonia absorbed; increase HE risk in cirrhosis
  • Benzodiazepines: associated with HE-related hospitalization, increased healthcare utilization
  • Gabapentin/GABA pathway medications: expected to increase HE risk
  • Zolpidem (sleep aid): associated with HE and falls in patients with HE
  • Proton pump inhibitors: numerous studies demonstrate increased HE risk (likely via gut microbiota alterations from gastric acid suppression)
  • Deprescribing should be coordinated multidisciplinary approach with informed patient discussion
Recompensation and Therapy Discontinuation
  • Patients who fully recompensate with cure/suppression of underlying cirrhosis etiology may no longer require secondary prophylaxis
  • Recompensation outside HE involves: resolution of ascites off diuretics, absence of variceal bleeding for 12 months, improvement in liver function (albumin, INR, bilirubin)
  • If patient has no signs/symptoms of HE while on therapy, HE-directed medications may be discontinued sequentially with close monitoring for recurrence
Adherence and Social Determinants
  • Adherence depends on social determinants of health, patient access to medications
  • Cost of rifaximin significant; assess affordability per patient
  • Brief lapses in rifaximin therapy associated with readmissions after initial HE hospitalization

###

13. Frailty, Sarcopenia, Nutrition, and HE
Recommendations (17–21)

Recommendation 17: Recommend protein intake target of 1.2–1.5 g/kg/d in outpatients with HE (strong recommendation, moderate certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 18: Recommend BCAA supplementation in individuals with HE if protein needs cannot be met by food alone (strong recommendation, moderate certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 19: Suggest a late-night snack for patients with cirrhosis to reduce frailty and HE (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 20: Suggest against protein restriction in patients with HE because it increases muscle breakdown and does not reduce duration of HE (conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 21: Suggest exercise interventions in patients with HE to reduce risk for falls, lower portal pressure, and increase capacity of skeletal muscle for ammonia metabolism (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Key Concepts (20–25)

Key Concept 20: All patients with HE (including MHE/CHE) should be evaluated for impaired muscle health/physical fitness using available tools (sarcopenia/myosteatosis, skeletal muscle strength, physical function, frailty, cardiorespiratory fitness) because these are all important targets of HE therapy

Key Concept 21: Patients with impaired muscle health/physical fitness in any clinical presentation (sarcopenia, physical frailty, deconditioning) and patients with myosteatosis (fat infiltration into muscle) have increased risk for HE

Key Concept 22: Critically ill patients with HE should receive up to 2 g/kg/d protein intake

Key Concept 23: Sarcopenia is not by itself indication or contraindication for TIPS, but could be associated with increased post-TIPS HE and post-TIPS mortality

Key Concept 24: Increases in ammonia levels after anaerobiosis have not been associated with elevated risk for HE and should not discourage clinicians from prescribing exercise for patients with cirrhosis

Key Concept 25: Patients with any form of HE may experience increased risk of exercise-induced injury. Supervision by exercise professionals, caregivers, or other surrogates is key to prevent exercise-related adverse events

Association Between Muscle Health and HE
  • 40%–50% of patients with HE have either sarcopenia or frailty (significantly higher than expected by chance)
  • Patients with cirrhosis and myosteatosis: 2 times more likely to have history of HE (32% vs. 15%)
  • Strong association between cognitive and physical decline at MHE/CHE stage; parallel and interdependent trajectories
  • Muscle-brain axis pathophysiology: hyperammonemia-related changes, alterations in microbiome (gut-muscle-brain axis), decreased glutamine synthetase scavenging of ammonia by skeletal muscle, chronic inflammation, micronutrient deficiency (L-carnitine, thiamine, leucine, zinc)
  • Impaired muscle health leads to reduced ammonia metabolism and hyperammonemia → further muscle loss
Assessment Tools for Muscle Health/Physical Fitness
Anatomic Sarcopenia and Myosteatosis
Test Parameter Benefits Caveats
CT/MRI from HCC surveillance Cross-sectional area/volume of muscles at L2/L3 (SMI), psoas (PMI) Accurate; SMI and PMI most commonly studied Radiation + CT; postprocessing cost
MAMC (mid-arm muscle circumference) Calculated from mid-arm circumference and triceps skinfold Bedside test Operator dependency; device cost
Skeletal Muscle Strength
Test Parameter Benefits Caveats
Handgrip Isometric strength Bedside test, easy to implement; device cost + Low handgrip associated with HE and ↑ risk of incidental OHE
Measures of Physical Performance
Test Parameter Benefits Caveats
Gait speed Usual walking speed over specific distance Bedside test, easy to implement Requires walking
Balance Time (≤10 sec) able to stand with feet together, tandem, semitandem Bedside test, easy to implement Requires ability to stand
Chair stands Time to execute chair stands or number per unit time Bedside test, easy to implement Requires ability to stand up
TUG (Timed Up and Go) Time to stand up, walk 3 m, return, sit Bedside test, easy to implement Requires ability to stand up
Composite Scores
Test Components Benefits Caveats
LFI (Liver Frailty Index) Handgrip + balance + chair stands Bedside test, improves with exercise Requires ability to stand; device cost
Protein and Nutritional Requirements
Protein Intake Targets
  • Cirrhosis results in state of accelerated starvation: reductions in hepatic glycogen reserves force body to rely on lipids and proteins for gluconeogenesis
  • Studies identify 1.2 g/kg protein per day as threshold for positive nitrogen balance
  • Target protein intake with dietitian support associated with improved muscle mass, less OHE and MHE
Late-Evening Snack (LES)
  • Fasting time should be minimized with frequent meals, LES, early breakfast
  • Associated with improved nitrogen balance, total body protein stores, quality of life, reduced HE (RR 0.46, p=0.002)
  • Suggested composition: combination of complex carbohydrates and protein
Branched-Chain Amino Acids (BCAAs)
  • Systematic review of 16 RCTs evaluating BCAA in HE (12 OHE, 4 MHE): significant reduction in HE over follow-up 1–104 weeks (RR 0.73)
  • Long-term BCAA supplementation not necessary unless needs cannot be met by food alone
  • Recent trial showed no major impact of BCAAs
Other Amino Acid Supplements
  • β-Hydroxy-β-methylbutyrate (HMB): preliminary studies show improvements
  • Acetyl-L-carnitine: evolving evidence for HE reduction
  • L-ornithine L-aspartate (LOLA): reduces blood ammonia; role in HE treatment
Protein Source Considerations
  • Vegetable-based protein: improvements in psychometric testing, beneficial EEG changes, reduced serum ammonia (small studies)
  • Substitution of single meat-based meal with vegan/vegetarian alternative associated with decreased postprandial ammonia
  • Pathophysiologic rationale: increased fiber and stool bulk, beneficial alterations in intestinal microbiota, increases in arginine facilitating urea synthesis
Exercise Interventions
Safety Considerations
  • Hyperammonemia is expected response to anaerobic exercise conditions (similar to hyperlactatemia)
  • No OHE episodes observed when studied systematically
  • Exercise clinical trials have not reported incidental OHE among exercisers, including patients with prior OHE or MHE
  • Narrative and systematic reviews deem exercise safe for decompensated cirrhosis with no increased risk for exercise-related injuries
  • Potential risk of self-injury from motor skills and response time limitations caused by HE; caregivers should provide support during exercise training
Benefits
  • 90% of exercise clinical trials included diet as cointervention; recommend coprescribing exercise with nutritional regimen
  • Three studies assessing hepatic hemodynamics before/after exercise + dietary intervention showed reduced hepatic venous pressure gradient
  • Lifestyle modification interventions stabilize portal hypertension, decrease clinical burden
  • Exercise exposes patients to comaneuvers supporting cognitive health: mental engagement, attention shifts, social contact, community exposure, navigation
  • Fatigue (associated with hyperammonemia) improved by exercise; also improves vitality and overall mental/physical health scores (SF-36)
Supervision Requirements
  • For patients with HE, caregivers must make themselves available and provide support during exercise training
  • Involvement increases adherence to exercise/dietary prescription while decreasing risk of self-injury/falls
Medical Treatments Under Investigation
Modality Potential Mechanism Stages Tested Current Status
Rifaximin solid soluble dispersion Altered microbial function, enhanced bile acid profiles Prevention of HE development Phase 3 topline results show no significant effect on first HE prevention
Fecal microbiota transplant Microbial structure/function change Prevention of HE recurrence Phase 1–2a; larger studies needed
Defined bacterial consortium (VE303) Microbial structure/function change Prevention of HE recurrence Phase 1
Rifamycin SV MMX Microbial structure/function change Minimal HE Phase 2; improved sarcopenia, inflammation, microbial profile but not cognitive testing
Probiotic mixtures Microbial structure/function change Minimal/covert HE; prevention of OHE Phase 2; not approved for HE
Lactobacillus GG Microbial structure/function change Minimal HE Phase 2; improved endotoxemia and microbial profile but not cognitive testing
LPCN 1148 Androgen agonist Prevention of HE recurrence Phase 2; only for men, reduced HE recurrence
Ornithine phenylacetate Ammonia scavenger Inpatients with HE Phase 2; not effective overall
Glycerol phenylbutyrate Ammonia scavenger Prevention of HE recurrence Phase 2; successful but not with concomitant rifaximin
IV Albumin Anti-inflammatory, reduce endothelial dysfunction Minimal/covert HE; inpatient HE; prevention of OHE Phases 2–3
Golexanolone GABA-A receptor-modulating steroid antagonist Minimal/covert HE Phase 2; reduced impact of sleepiness and fatigue
14. HE in the Context of TIPS
Recommendations (22–23)

Recommendation 22: Recommend initiating rifaximin therapy 14 days before elective TIPS insertion and continuing for at least 6 months in patients with decompensated cirrhosis with or without prior episode of OHE to decrease risk of recurrent or de novo OHE (strong recommendation, moderate certainty of evidence)

Recommendation 23: Suggest embolizing extrahepatic collaterals at time of TIPS to reduce post-TIPS HE (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Post-TIPS HE Epidemiology
  • Occurs in 30%–40% of patients after TIPS
  • Associated with poor HRQoL and prognosis
  • Refractory post-TIPS HE occurs in 3%–7% of patients; most cases when portal-systemic gradient decreases >60% post-TIPS
Risk Factors for Post-TIPS HE
  • Increased age
  • Worse liver function
  • Hyponatremia
  • Pre-TIPS SPSSs
  • HE history
  • Larger stent diameter
  • Increased portal pressure gradient reduction
  • Sarcopenia
Impact of Sarcopenia on Post-TIPS HE
  • Substantial literature evaluating impact
  • TIPS can result in improvements in muscle mass (portal pressure reduction, ascites resolution, improved dietary absorption)
  • Patients who improve sarcopenia post-TIPS have lower risk of HE post-procedure
  • Body fat distribution may also play role: increased risk with alterations in body fat distribution pre-TIPS (low visceral fat in males, low subcutaneous fat in females); lower risk with improvements in subcutaneous adipose tissue index
Prevention Strategies
  • Lactulose/lactitol: smaller studies unsuccessful vs. placebo
  • Embolization of extrahepatic collaterals: may decrease post-TIPS HE
  • Prophylactic rifaximin (14 days pre-TIPS, 6 months post): significantly reduced post-TIPS HE occurrence in RCT (mostly refractory ascites patients with alcohol etiology)
Treatment of Post-TIPS HE
  • Similar to non-TIPS HE: lactulose and rifaximin
  • If unsuccessful or refractory: reducing TIPS diameter through ballooning or placing smaller TIPS within TIPS can dramatically reduce diameter, improve HE
  • If clinically appropriate, consider liver transplant
15. Liver Transplant and HE
Recommendation (24)

Recommendation 24: For patients with multiple HE episodes and MELD score <15, suggest evaluating candidacy for living donor liver transplantation (conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence)

Key Concepts (26–29)

Key Concept 26: Adding 4–5 MELD points regardless of MELD score iteration used more accurately reflects 90-day mortality of inpatient OHE and outpatient CHE

Key Concept 27: As multiple HE episodes lead to persistent cognitive impairment which may not recover after LT, early LT could have additional benefits to improve brain function

Key Concept 28: Early evaluation for liver transplantation should be considered in patients with high MELD3.0 scores, severe HE (grades III–IV), or frequent HE episodes to improve long-term outcomes

Key Concept 29: Persistent confusion in inpatient setting is relative contraindication to transplant

HE and Transplant Listing Criteria
  • Since changeover from Child score to MELD and subsequent iterations, HE is not given extra priority points
  • Latest iteration (MELD3.0) still does not take HE into account for organ allocation
  • This omission could lead to underestimation of true illness severity in patients with HE regarding prioritization on transplant waitlist
Evidence Supporting HE Impact on Mortality
  • Prior guidelines: HE alone not considered indication for LT unless associated with poor liver function
  • Severe HE (grades 3–4) significantly increases 90-day waitlist mortality, independent of MELD score
  • Patients with MELD scores 30–34 and severe HE have similar mortality rates to those with MELD scores ≥35 without HE
  • Studies support addition of 7 points (Lucidi et al) or 4–5 points (Silvey et al) to MELD for patients with documented inpatient OHE
CHE Impact on Prognosis
  • Independently associated with poor survival, increased risk of hospitalization
  • Higher risk of developing OHE, hospitalization, death or transplant even after controlling for MELD score
  • Additional MELD points needed to counter impact of HE exclusion
Impact of Pretransplant HE on Post-Transplant Outcomes
  • Multiple HE episodes lead to cumulative and potentially irreversible cognitive deficits
  • Patients with severe pretransplant HE (grades 3–4) have lower overall post-LT survival, primarily within first year
  • Increased mortality largely attributed to higher rates of infection-related deaths
  • Severe pretransplant HE associated with longer hospital stays post-LT (independent of MELD score) due to higher postoperative complications and morbidity
  • Neurological complications more common: higher risk for perioperative events (altered mental status, seizures)
  • Persistent deficit may remain in patients with history of OHE pre-LT particularly in attention, executive function, psychomotor speed
  • Overall brain volume and function in pretransplant HE associated with reduced brain volume and neuronal markers, may not fully normalize post-LT
  • Cognitive inflexibility and learning deficits may improve post-LT but may not reach levels seen in patients without HE
Living Donor Liver Transplantation (LDLT)
  • Offers survival benefit for patients with low MELD scores
  • Particularly relevant for patients with recurrent HE because timely transplantation can prevent further cognitive decline, improve overall prognosis
  • Early transplantation in patients with frequent HE episodes mitigates high morbidity associated with recurrent HE → better long-term cognitive outcomes and quality of life
  • Should be considered for patients with multiple episodes of HE and low MELD scores
Operationalizing HE Diagnosis for Transplant Prioritization
  • Difficult due to subjective nature of diagnosis
  • Studies used cognitive testing and evidence of hospitalizations as benchmarks
  • More data needed to address potential gaming if additional points awarded
  • Validated, reproducible inpatient diagnosis needed for objectivity; natural language processing shows promise
  • Patients whose confusion does not improve despite adequate treatment likely not appropriate candidates for LT due to high potential for persistently poor brain recovery post-LT
16. Conclusions
Key Takeaways
  • HE in cirrhosis is a multiorgan disorder of gut-brain axis
  • Major negative impact on: patients, caregivers, medical systems, society
  • Pathogenesis, symptoms, effect on clinical/psychosocial function, and need for multidisciplinary approach highlighted
  • Current diagnostic and management strategies have made major strides
  • Gaps remain in: appropriate identification of HE, treatment of patients not responsive to available therapies
  • Impact of HE on liver transplant eligibility needs further elucidation
Emerging Therapeutic Targets
  • Gut-brain axis: FMT, defined bacterial consortia, rifaximin formulations
  • Sarcopenia: exercise interventions, nutritional support, androgen agonists (LPCN 1148)
  • Ammonia: ammonia scavengers (ornithine phenylacetate, glycerol phenylbutyrate)
  • Brain neurosteroids: golexanolone (GABA-A receptor-modulating steroid antagonist)
Future Directions
  • Novel targets focused on gut-brain axis, sarcopenia, ammonia, and brain neurosteroids being investigated
  • Need for better diagnostic tools for CHE/MHE
  • Need for therapies for patients with refractory HE
  • Need to address impact of HE on transplant allocation
